PREFACE.

HE present number of the “ Gaelic Athletic Annual” is

the gratifying outcome of the support and commenda-

tion bestowed upon preceding issues. The work has now, for

the first time, received the formal approval and support of the

National Athletic Association; and, consequently, while its

future is secured, its importance and influence are correspond-
ingly increased.

In every respect, this issue will be found an advance upon
its predecessors. Those who initiated the publication promised
Gaels that, if guaranteed a moderate amount of co-operation,
they would provide a record and review of Gaelic Athletic
affairs worthy of the athletic prestige, vitality, and ambitions of
our race.

The needed assurances were generously given, and this is
the promised outcome of our labours. We trust it will be con-
sidered commensurable with our professions, and with the
anticipations of our friends.

With regard to the various articles, we will, as heretofore,
remain impartially silent, leaving to Gaeldom at large the final
judgment.

‘We must, however, thank our contributors for their aid in

- making the “ Annual” not alone a repository of Gaelic ideals,

but a practical review of all Gaelic matters of importance.

To the Councils and individual Gaels who assisted in the
continued publication of the “ Annual,” we desire to express our
hearty acknowledgments.

The winners of the Athletic Championships up to date
have been included in this issue, those from 1885 to 1906 being
reprinted from the “ Irish Athletic Record,” by the kind permis-
sion of the Editor.

The “Annual ” is now in the hands of Gaels for the third
time—theirs to maintain and develop in its original and still
primary object—the conservation of the active traditions of
Irish manhood. Al else is transitory and futile and delusive.
In so far as this aim has been upheld, and in so far as its main-
tenance is safeguarded, let the “ Annual” flourish.

America, with the aid of Irish material, outclasses the

3

world in individual athletics. Training is very hard, and even
during the winter months college students go daily through
prescribed forms of exercise. In events which demand most
skill and application, the laurels will shortly be only for
students, as athletes obliged to work for a living cannot afford
to devote sufficient time to train. Even now there are practi-
cally no athletes proficient at hurdles except collegians. Shot-
putting comes in the same category, for it requires more skill
than any of the other weight events.

It is doubtful if a man is repaid for the trouble involved
in training to negotiate ten hurdles three feet six inches high
in fifteen seconds. .

Irish shot-putters have no trainers. Denis Horgan was a
remarkable athlete, for he had never seen a better man than
himself in action until he had reached his prime, yet he held a
world’s record for years.

When England discovered the use of steel spikes for run-
ning shoes, it almost stood her as well as when she applied
steam to develop industries. It gave her an undoubted start in
the game. Now she has no men for real athletic events, except
a few distance runners, and even they have to give way to
Swedes and Italians, and England suffers Frenchmen to win her
big cross-country races.

This branch of sport was wisely taken hold of by the G.A.A.
some years ago. Irishmen are natural jumpers, and over a real
country with ‘fairly stiff fences, no English team could finish
in front of them. Years ago when the English-Trish runners
met an English cross-country team to run an international
match over a course in the Dublin suburbs (Baldoyle), the
Englishmen did not like the size of the fences, and decided to
withdraw if they were not lowered. Imagine Englishmen in
England being asked to conform to such a request. Yet, on
the authority of a well-known runner, the fences were lowered,
and the Englishmen won when the route was made ready for
their running.

IRISH ATHLETES IN AMERICA.

Irish Athletes in America have attracted admiration for our
race, and their feats have raised Irishmen higher in the estima-
tion of other peoples. In the Spanish mountains youths set

4
their hearts on the Bull Ring at Madrid, and in, all’ the
Spanish-American countries, aspirants for fame at Spa%n's
national pastime have similar longings. So, too, the grouping
of first-class exponents of amateur athletics in New York has
made Manhattan Island the Mecca of young Irish athletes.
There is really little athletics in America outside the colleges and
the National Guard Regiments. Irish athletes, or men who have
not had early experience of board floor running (as most of the
races are so run in New York), seldom attain the proficiency

associated with their work on out-door tracks ~ The same

The Irishmen who win honours here will_in a few years
hence, be rated as Americans, or simply as point scorers for

mercenaries in continental armies, as were the Swiss and

‘Americans will read how the “sturdy Saxon,” Flanagan, really
introduced hammer-throwing. Surely Irish athletic ability is
a jewel of which any nation should be proud, but that jewel
would look far better on the bosom of the Queen to whom if
rightly belongs.

were fruitless. At the London meet the athletes of the Insh
Amateur Athletic Association had their abilities deflected
towards the accumulation of the meagre English score. It will
be recollected that prior to this meet, the G.A.A. made applica-
tion to the promoters of the London Olympiad for its athletes
to compete as Irishmen for Ireland. On being informed that
Treland was a province of England in athletics, and that she
would not be recognised as a nation, the G.A.A. would not
acquiesce in such a humiliation, and properly refrained from
participation.

The Irish athletes, who won world fame and records, have
unfortunately received little support from their national
athletic organisation. During the period of weakness the
G.AA. perfected a mutual agreement with the Irish A.AA,
but such an unnational alliance could not last. The Gaelic
Language Revival, and the healthy national spirit which it
infused into Ireland, induced the G.A.A. to object to English
soldiers and police in their games. The LA.AA, guardian of
the English idea and interest, retaliated, and the two athletic
organisations immediately drifted apart. Purely as a sporting
issue, the action of the G.A.A. may appear narrow, and needs
explanation.  Gaudily-dressed recruiting sergeants made a
practice of attending Gaelic athletic meets with a view to entice
the athletes and desirable youths into the English military
service. Ireland is proud of her sons who fought for freedom
anywhere, but England only wars now on unarmed or defence-
less coloured peoples. The G.A.A. simply decided to save its
members from the brand of murderers.

AN AMERICAN ATHLETIC ALLIANCE

At England’s best, she had no right to dictate athletics to
Ireland. Now, when her scientists and public men admit that
England “rots at the marrow,” the course of the Gaelic Athletic
Association in entering into an agreement with America as the
chief exponent of amateur athletics, is clear. The “ 56" and the
All-round Competitions have never been held in England. The
Inter-'Varsity contests between Oxford and Cambridge would
eliminate the weights but for the American students, who take
the Rhodes scholarships. Lovers of Irish sports and pastimes

the first Gaelic athletic individual championships at which the
twenty weights, jumps and runs of the Irish programme should
be contested. The G.A.A. was primarily conceived to benefit
Irishmen in Ireland, and the fame of American renown should
not entice Irish athletes to this country. The fact that an Irish-
man shows distinct athletic ability makes him a mark for
emigration. The drain of the brawniest and best men from
Ireland is sad to contemplate. . Even should Ireland enjoy
freedom, it is certain there would be some emigration to this
country. But American Gaelic clubs should be suspended for
paying the expenses of any Irish athlete to America. It is
unsportsmanlike as well as unnatural, as these contests should
be for recreation between men who desire air and exercise on
Sundays after a week’s confined work in shop or factory.
Instead they have resolved into exhibitions of crack players
among whom the average athlete appears a novice. Ireland is
paid for her cattle, sheep and horses, but she receives nothing
for her athletes, the best men on God’s creation.

By adhering to intelligent rules and by living up to the
ideals of Irish sportsmanship, which is recognised the world
over in games as divergent as handball and horse-racing, Gaelic
pastimes in America should enjoy a great future, and ties with
the motherland can become more loving and enduring,

Upon his native sward the Hurler stands
To play the ancient pastime of the Gael,

KERRY IN THE FOOTBALL CHAMPIONSHIPS.
By AUSTIN STACK.

T is a long cry back to the time when the Killorglin Laune

Rangers mopopolised the Kerry Football Championships.

| This was when the Association was first started in Kerry. Later

the game in the county saw very low water. The County Board

ceased to exist about 1897, and there were no championships for
two or three years.

A new era dawned in 1900; that year saw the birth of the
team which has since gained so much renown in the football
field. The Killorglin men, following in the footsteps of the old
“Rangers ” (some of whom turned out with the youngsters), won
the 1900 Championship after a hard tussle with Killarney. The
Tralee John Mitchels, though affiliated, did not compete owing
to a misunderstanding. The 1901 Championship proved very
interesting. Killarney defeated Killorglin early by a small score.
The Rangers had previously got the verdict over Killarney in the
O'Connell Cup.  Killorglin then met Tralee in the Final, and,
to the surprise of everyone (except Trale¢ men, possibly), Tralee
simply romped home from their opponents. The Championship
Final created great interest throughout, and was witnessed (for
a county match) by a tremendous concourse of spectators, Kil-
larney coming out victors on a score of eight points to seven.
The 1902 Championship saw Tralee getting their revenge, when

_ they beat Killarney by four points after a most exciting game.
Until a year or two back, these teams always met in the Cham-
pionship, and more than once a draw has been the result. The
rivalry between them was most keen, and the players trained
hard to acquire every art to win for their respective clubs. Some-
thing, whether good luck or else, has, however, kept the Cham-
pionship in Tralee, and, as the record shows, the Mitchels have
now held the Championship continuously since 1902. The
matches between Tralee and Killarney have brought about
greater things than the winning of County Championships, for
it was in these games that the men attained that degree of pro-
ficiency which won them their first All-Ireland Championship.

"

They have handed down all their skill and prowess to the
younger generation, who have proved themselves worthy succes-
Sors of the men who made battle against Kildare at Tipperary
and Cork, and again at Cork for the 1903 Final. But this is
anticipating things. For the first two years after the resurrec-
tion of the G.A.A. in Kerry, the Inter-County Football Teams
performed miserably. The first County team went down
before the Clonmel Shamrocks at Limerick, and in the following
year Cork (Nils) defeated Kerry at Millstreet. The tault
seemed to be in the mode of selection, and want of cohesl«?n
amongst the players. It was a case of so many men from this-
that, and the other place, without due regard to the proper
placing cf the units. o

The year 10903 saw things somewhat improved in this
respect.  The Selection Committee decided—wisely, as events
proved—to confine the pick as closely as possible to the Tralee
and Killarney teams. They did so, and got to the Munster
Final, in which they met Tipperary at Turner's Cross (Cork).
The Kerrymen were very unlucky not to win this match, and
they were more unlucky still to loge the decider, for they had
to do without the services of two of their best men. Tipperary
pulled through by a point, but subsequently lost the All-Ireland
Final to the Isles of the Sea by a narrow margin. The Kerry-
men made no mistake in the next year’s Munster Championship,
in the Final of which they beat the Lees (Cork) by a substantial
score. Next to go under to them were Mayo in the Provincial
Semi-Final, and then it was a question whether the Munster
Champions would be opposed by Kildare or Kilkenny for the
Home Final. It will be remembered these teams had to meet
three times before deciding the Leinster Championship. Even-
tually, Kildare came out victors.

Vivid are my recollections of that July day at Tipperary
when Kildare and Kerry met for the first time. ~ The result
was voted a certainty almost for Kildare; for had they not
smothered the Dublin Champions before beating Kilkenny ?
Half the male population of the kingdom must have travelled
to Tipperary on that day, and the welkin rang with the rival
war-cries.  The slogan, “Up, Kerry,” may be said to have
been born at that match. “Go on, Short Grass,” was the
counter cry.  There is no doubt the Kildare men anticipated

15

aneasy win.  Their players were trained to perfection—in fact
they were at their very best. In the writer’s opinion, they went
back in condition after that match, and never again did they
shov!{ anything like the same form. They could not stand the
continuous training since the previous autumn. The Kerrymen,
on the other hand, had had no match from October to April, and
so had room for improvement. Besides, they were the younger
lot, and they had more dash in them. But I must say something
about my impressions of the game played that memorable day.
1 have seen many a football match, but never anything like the
play witnessed during the first half hour. The fielding was
superb, the combination grand, and the pace terrific. Not a
spectator who was not roused to the highest pitch of excitement
an§ enthusiasm. Half time came with Kildare leading by two
points to one.  They had been playing with the incline for the
flrlst period.  The restart saw Kerry equalising after a few
minutes.  Kildare responds with another point, Kerry again

draws level. Here occurs an interruption. Kildare scores a

goal after the ball has struck a spectator on the sideline. Kerry

protests, but the score is allowed. No score for a long time.

It seems hours to the Kerry supporters. Then a point is

obtained after storming Kildare’s lines for ever so long. Only

two points now, boys! Oh, for a goal! Only two minutes
to full time! A “free” to Kerry. The Kerrymen surround

Fhe Kildare posts. The kick is well directed, and lands just

in front of the goal. A scramble. No one can say what has

happened.  The green flag is raised. A goal—can it be pos-
sible? The Kildare men object. ~The spectators crowd in;
* no more football for that day.
Cork is the next venue. Kerry show great improvement,
and Kildare are lucky to make a draw.
The teams met again in Cork on 15th October, 1905, and

Kerry won by seven points to four.

) Thus ended the three great games between the representa-
tives of the “ Kingdom ” and the County of the “ Short Grass.”
The match certainly created more interest than any other held
under the auspices of the G.A.A. before or since, and the skill
and sportsmanlike conduct of the players did more than any-
thing else has done to raise the morale of Gaelic players and
the status of the Gaelic code. Following up their victory over

10

Kildare, the Kerrymen defeated the London Irish at Jones’s
Road, and landed their first All-Ireland Championship.  The
players who took part in the final match against Kildare were :

—T. O’Gorman (Capt.), J. O'Gorman, M. McCarthy, C. Healy,

J. T. Fitzgerald, J. Buckley, D. Curran, A. Stack (Tralee), P.
Dillon (goal), R. Fitzgerald, W. Lynch, J. Myers, D. McCarthy,
D. Kissane (Killarney), R. Kirwan, D. Breen (Castleisland), and
E O'Neill (Valentia).

F. O’Sullivan (Killarney), and P. J. Cahill (Tralee), also
participated in other matches during the 1903 Championship.

Kerry again won the Munster Championship in 1904, beat

Ulster in the Semi-Final, and met the C. J. Kickhams of Dublin
for the Final. The Kickhams had a great combination that
year, and were strong favourites. One of the reasons for this
was that the Kerry players were not well prepared, and it was
thought, up to a week before the match, that several of the team
would cry off. All turned up, however, at Cork, and Kerry won
fairly easily. )

The team was constituted as follows : —A. Stack (Capt.), M.
McCarty, John T. Fitzgerald, J. O’Gorman, T. O’Gorman, C.
Healy, P. J. Cahill, J. Buckley, D. Curran, John O’Sullivan
(Tralee), P. Dillon (goal), J. Myers, D. McCarthy, R. Fitz-
gerald, W. Lynch, F. O’Sullivan (Killarney), D: Breen (Castle-
island). .

After this, the 1905 Championship looked a certainty for
Kerry, who ran through the Munster and Semi-Final ties with
flying colours. Kildare were again to represent Leinster.
There was much delay concerning the venue, and eventually the
Central Council ordered the match to be played at Thurles.
Meantime a few of the Kerry players had gone to America,
but when the men lined out, the team seemed to be a good one.
It did not prove to be good enough for Kildare, and the latter
won comfortably after a fine struggle.

With the 1906 and 1907 Finals, Kerry had nothing to'say,
as they were knocked out in the Provincial ties.

The 1908 Championship saw Kerry again in the Final, and
again at Thurles, which, for the second time, proved their
Waterloo. The Dublin Geraldines were the winners on this
occasion.

TWENTY.THREE YEARS AFTER.

17

The 19og Championship is still fresh in the minds of the
Gaels of Ireland, who saw the boys from the extreme South-
West fighting out the Final against gallant little Louth, who
had not figured in a final as Provincial representatives since the
1887 Championship. The match drew a record crowd to Jones’s
Road, and a great game was witnessed, the verdict going to
Kerry.

The players on that occasion were:—T. Costello (Capt.),.
Maurice McCarthy, F. J. Cronin, T. Rice, C. Healy, J. O’Sulli-
van, M. J. Quinlan, D. Breen (Tralee), P. Dillon (goal), R. Fitz-
gerald, j. Skinner, E. Spillane, C. Murphy (Killarney), J.
McCarthy (Valentia), J. Kennelly, E. Mullane (Listowel), and
B. O’Connor (St. Brendan’s, Dingle).

Such, in brief, is an outline of Kerry’s football progress
and achievements.

THE WORK OF THE G.A.A. IN LOUTH.

OUTH is, perhaps, one of the most progressive Gaelic
counties in the country. Coming into the ranks of the
Association at an early date, the county made its mark in the
very first All-Ireland Championship, and though the game sub-
sequently declined and we had more than a decade of inactivity,
yet since the revival in 1902 progress has been steady, and but
for internal dissensions Leinster senior football honours would
have crossed the Boyne long before the year 19og. Dissension
is not a pleasant subject to dwell upon. Fortunately, to-day it
is but an unhappy remembrance of other times, and with both
ends of the county working in the most complete unanimity the
prospects for the future are of the brightest. In Louth it has
been long recognised that certain areas require special grading,
and in order to encourage teams from the rural districts, the
Committee have started an Intermediate Competition. I do not
think many other countries have yet discriminated so far between
their junior and senior material, but I do know that if the spirit
of the Louth County Board’s idea is carried into proper effect, it
is bound to have a wholesome influence on the power of the
Association in the country districts. County clubs are recruited
from such a wide area, have such poor facilities for training and
lose players so frequently by emigration'and other causes, that
it is impossible to ekpect them to compete on an equality with
their friends from the towns who have their players close at
hand, and generally experience little difficulty in procuring
training quarters. It was chiefly as an incentive to those clubs
that the Louth Board inaugurated this grade, and up to the
present it has worked remarkably well.

The League system has also proved of advantage, and the
competition which started in 1906-7, and merged subsequently in
the Healy Cup Competition, proved of immense benefit to the
players.

The winning of the Leinster Championship in 1909
certainly gave a great impetus to the game in the county, though
the subsequent defeat by Kerry came as a disappointment to
many Louth Gaels, and here let me add, that while our players
have nothing to learn from any other county either in style,
combination or general adaptability, still they always leave an

‘impression on the mind of the onlooker that in speed and

26

general fitness they lack essential qualities for continued
success. These are faults which have been pointed out over and
over again to the players, and yet with every possible facility
for perfect training both in Dundalk and Drogheda, those
faults are still the predominant feature of the Louth players in
Inter-County contests. They must permanently disappear
before we can hope to maintain our highest prestige in the future.
It is most gratifying to have to record that the support
accorded to the Association in Louth both by the press and
public is of the most whole-hearted kind. The press has always
been at the disposal of the promoters of the GAA, and can
claim no little credit for the successful work of the Association
in the county; while the public have always attended in large
numbers at the various games. It is mainly owing to these joint
influences that foreign games are almost exclusively confined to
the “ garrison.” In fact, for all practical purposes, the games
promoted by the G.A.A. are the only games alive in the county
to-day, and there are few districts between Dundalk and
Drogheda which are not represented at the County Board.

It is unfortunate that hurling languishes, and all attempts
made to create interest in the game seem to fail. At present
there are four clubs affiliated, and if the year is as successful as
anticipated, the Committee may take a practical step to spread
local interest in the finest games under the zgis of the G.A.A.
The popularity of football is unbounded, and almost every
other branch of sport is subordinated to the love of the round
leather ball. A handball championship was held in 1909, and
proved fairly successful, but athletics are not flourishing by any
means.  The mediocrity of our runners and cyclists is most
marked compared to the band of local men who kept athletics in
full tune about a decade ago. In those years sports were held
all over the county. Drogheda, Ardee, Dunleer and Dundalk
never missed their summer meeting. Dundalk alone remains,
and local men are seldom notably successful. Good local men
have always a drawing power with local people, and without
strong home support athletics—of all branches of sport—cannot
thrive.  Just now there is'a prospect of a revival. Harrier
clubs have become quite numerous, but the future is as yet so
uncertain that it is not worthy of more than passing notice.

On the whole, the Gaels of Louth have little cause to
grumble at their present position and future prospects.

THE CORK GAME dF BOWLS.
By “ CARBERY.”

WITH the single exception of our own inimitable game of
ash and leather, there is no pastime so distinctive and so
peculiarly characteristic of the qualities of our race as the Cork
game of bowling.  To excel at bowls a man must be strong,
very supple, keen of eye, sound in judgment, and active in every
sinew and in every limb from finger-tip to toe. Surely such a
game, which, in spite of adverse laws and without even the
semblance of a board of control, continues to fascinate
thousands weekly in our largest county, is deserving of some
attention and of a short space at least in the ANNUAL of our
Gaelic pastimes.
Of the origin of the game very little is known, and in look-
ing up old and modern works touching the social habits and
amusements of our people, I have found no mention of the
pastime. But for us it is good enough to know that our great
grandfathers played the game in Cork, Waterford and Clare
(with much larger bowls, it is true, than those now used), but
they played their { score ” very much as we play it now all over
the centre, south and west of County Cork. One of my earliest
recollections is to listen with all the fervid interest of childhood
to a very old man in my neighbourhood, when referring to the
decadence of these later days, tell of how the young men of his
time divided their year’s play between “ three menths’ bowling
and nine months’ goaling.” I wished I lived in those days!
He told of the big scores between Hickey and McCarthy when
there were a hundred horsemen with loaded whips to keep the
road clear. Of the famous Billy Murphy that used to play under-
hand, and land a bow five times in succession into a wicker-work
pannier eighty yards away. Of the enchanted “ Fachna” who
could threw as “long a bowl” up a hill as down, and whom no
man living had the courage to play a score against. Tales of
such nature, bearing the traces of exaggeration that tradition
alone can bestow, serve to indicate that for antiquity, too, the
game of bowls comes next to the good old goaling garme.

28

As many readers will know nothing whatever of the game,
1 shall give a brief account of how it is played. A pair of solid
iron bowls, about twenty-eight ounces each in weight, form the
sole requirements. Given a quiet road with a moderately good
surface, two young men on the look-out for a healthy and fas-
cinating day’s pleasure can start away at once. They start
from an appointed mark, keeping a look-out for passers-by, and
agree to finish at a familiar gate-pillar, at a cross-roads, or
at a farmer's house—whatever comes the handiest—say half a
mile ahead. Each throws, the object being to send the bowl as
far along the road as possible. They start the second throw
from the end of the first, and so on, the man who gets to the
score’s end in the least number of throws winning. Generally,
however, a match is made, and there are two partners against
two opposing partners. This gives increased zest to the game,
as co-operation is essential to success then, and the glorious
uncertainty is doubled. Generally there is a small bet on the
score, but as score follows score usually with varying success,
very little is lost or won. But big stakes are occasionally
played for between first-class players, and between champions
of different baronies or different towns, and on these occasions
the enthusiasm and the excitement of the respective followers
know no bounds. The roads are black with people, and occa-
sionally unpleasant scenes occur. It is here the necessity for
control comes in—but of this more anon.

Tt is clear, from the above brief description, that strength
and accuracy are the two outstanding factors in producing a
good bowler. To travel far especially on heavy roads, the bowl
must leave the hand at a high speed, and in order to get the full
benefit of this speed the bowl must land on that part of the road
which will tend to keep it for as long a period as possible on the
hard surface. The part where the bowl lands first is called the
“play,” and if a bowl is placed accurately and with good
strength, a good throw is the usual result. Some first-class men
can strike the little tuft of grass sixty yards away that their
advisers put down for their guidance. This will give some idea
of the perfection to which the pastime has been brought.
All players swing the arm completely around after a run
of eight or ten yards, and much depends on the method
which a player has acquired in performing this run up

20

and arm swing. Ease and grace of delivery invariably tell in
the course of a long day’s playing. Then good judgment is
needed to know when to “loft” a bowl far, and when to play
low and smoothly; when to bring a bowl to the hip, and when
to keep the swing far out to secure the “side” that carries the
bowl around curves. These and countless other minor points
make bowling a fine art and a game in which proficiency
demands long study and practice. But for amusement and
health a pair of the veriest novices, closely matched, will enjoy
a quiet score as much as a pair of champions. This makes the
game extremely popular in country places during the long
Sunday evenings in summer. Countless happy hours have I
spent this wise in my ’teens, and I love a score to-day more than
ever.. The walking involved brings abundant health, the com-
petition is exhilarating, the pleasure of seeing a bowl speeding
alfmg the bright, hard road is keen to a degree, and the con-
sciousness of being “ up against” a man just as good brings out
the best and most manly qualities in our nature. Yes, properly
organised and properly controlled, bowling would soon be
universally popular, and would become as important a factor
to\!_lards increasing the pleasures of rural life all over Ireland
as it unquestionably is to-day in those comparatively small areas
which are its home.

Any article on bowl-playing would be incomplete without
making mention of the leading lights in the game in modern
years. The great Owen Egan was perhaps the first of these
great later-day players. A nice tidy player, very accurate and
?f a stout heart, his games with Mahony created enormous
interest. Later on came the big matches between “Scotty”
Leahy, of Bandon, and “Black” Coleman, Blackpool, on the
Clancoole Road.  The bowling on this occasion reached a
very high standard, and “lofts” were thrown that have never
since been excelled. So keen was the competition between these
men and so insuppressible their love for the old game, that when.
in later years they went the way many of the best of our race
go—to the land of Stars and Stripes—they met again in the
presence of many cheering fellow-exiles, and played once more,
four thousand miles away from Bandon Bridge.

30

Perhaps the greatest bowling figure in recent years was
“Buck” McGrath, of Murphy’s Brewery, Cork. He certainly
had the biggest heart of any man that ever played. Hard luck
in the early part of a contest troubled him not, in fact it only
made him put greater heart into his game. Again and again in
his big “ scores ” he smiled with honest confidence in the face of
the mighty handicap of two bowls odds half-way through,
and it was often mn the last throw that he landed “the
goods” for his adoring backers. His matches are well
remembered by all followers of the game south of the
Blackwater. “Buck,” too, has found his way to and con-
tinues his fame in, Yankee land. When McGrath was in his
prime he met with an unexpected defeat in a big score with a
fine cut of a young bowler—Jim Barrett, of the south side.
Barrett stood six fect one, and was an Adonis in mould. He
was cool, canny, strong, and just the man to lower McGrath’s
colours. Barrett continued to win everywhere after McGrath
left Ireland, and landed no less than twelve successive “big”
scores inside a few years. Then came the one and only Paddy
Murphy, of Waterloo, a young giant of six feet three in his
stockinged feet, with a style that combined ease and strength
to a wonderful degree, and with a loft that only Driscoll in his
prime could equal. Though Barrett’s greater experience told in
the early matches, Murphy’s shyness gradually wore away, and
in the last big “score” played in the Cork liberties he proved
himself a real champion on a road that suited him but little.
Murphy is still the generally acknowledged bowling champion,
and with his privilege of venue (for he will now chose a wide
road) he is not likely to be beaten for years. The only likely
candidates for championship honours outside the veteran Jim
Barrett are the well-known athletes, W. F. Bennett, D. O'Leary,
Coppeen, and Jack Driscoll, of Clondrohid. All three are first-
rate men, and have given good accounts of their worth. I
should dearly like to see Bennett and Murphy meet in the near
future for championship honours. It will be a contest, for the
Ballinhassig all-round performer is perhaps the most lion-
hearted man since “ Buck” McGrath’s time.

It is pretty clear that the time is ripe for controlling the
game. The first step is to secure the County Councils’ sanction
over certain roads at stated times. Then an energetic com-

31

mittee, under the wing of the G.A.A., could draft rules and
arrange for trophies in a healthy and legitimate fashion. It
only needs an enthusiast to take the lead; the game is there; the
game is good, and its popularity would mean much for the rural
populace, whose range of amusements is indeed narrow. It
would make them healthy ; it would keep them out of the tap-
rooms; it would give them an interest in life, and would help
to keep them what and where they ought to be—a vigorous and
contented peasantry in their own land.

There are many practical, commonplace, even mercenary

ON A POINT OF ORDER.

To a keen observer of the routine of Gaelic athletic affairs
and to one appreciating the value of method in the discharge of
even the most primitive functions, the want of system, regu-
larity, co-ordination, call it what you will, is deplorable. But
its essential fault is that it is easily avoidable. Trace it from
the first frivolous objection in the initial round of the Annual
Championship; follow it through the vacillation of the Com-
mittee and the wire-pulling of partisans; behold it in the ulti-
mate appeal in a dying rehearsal of all its earlier involutions,

39 #

and you have in nine cases out of ten the cause of late conven-
tions, unfinished championships, disorganised teams and dis-
gusted patrons.  Without depriving any team of legitimate
rights, there must be a restriction upon the originating causes
and an acceleration in the subsequent stages of such disputes.
Justice, if she must be blind (a truly English fable), need not
surely totter on a stick or have to be borne on a stretcher.

But, you will say, what of the wire-pulling, the partisans,
the blindness to real interests and elementary duty towards the
Association and game? It is a topic for serious consideration,
a problem for a subsequent wrestle. We will only preface the
conclusion by saying that the consciously right on our Gaelic
Boards are not always strong enough in their faith, and so
audacity and compromise succeed where they should never
obtrude.

To look to the brighter picture—when abuses and mis-
management will have practically disappeared : what an induce-
ment to support; what a facility for earnest zeal ; what an index
of capacity and progressive strength would not be revealed by
the inauguration of regularity in the opening and ending of
championships, the holding of conventions, the season for
athletic carnivals, tournaments, and the various minutiz that
go to fill up, but generally submerge, the present activities of
official Gaels? 7

Some cynic says: “Get angels on the Boards!” No, they
are too intangible. “Get Gaels” say I—honest, energetic
Gaels, imbued with a sense of order and responsibility, dignity
and spirit, and the cause of Gaelic games will march forward
like a well-disciplined host.

One can therefore welcome the recent regulations to penalise
frivolous objections and compel the despatch of lagging cham-
pionships. ~ We look, however, to manifestations of the spirit
rather than to the operation of such laws; for if the Gaels in
office and in the field require a punitive stimulus to rise to a
proper sense of what is due to their games, the most drastic
enactments become futile, or are vindicated only in the destruc-
tion of the interests they were designed to serve.

PROCEDURE AND PERSONNEL.

The consideration of such a topic carries one at once to the
bodies where rules and bye-laws are made and supposed to te
administered. In the personnel and procedures of some can be
found a source of many evils that beset the progress of native
games. It would be unjust to judge many, even of the most
unsatisfactory members, of Gaelic Councils by their actions and
assertions alone. A tendency towards irrational views irduce
many to run counter to principle, reality and common sense, not
with a desire to damage, but for self-distinction. However,
nothing can palliate such outbursts of vain opposition and
destructive parochialism as are frequently displayed. It is
not unjust to say that men wanting in loyalty to the aims they
ostensibly uphold, and who would to secure a transient success
injure permanent interests, and the supreme ideal of the move-
ment, are dangerous to the Association and all it seeks to
conserve.

It would be futile to deny that such undesirable qualities
were apparent even at the last All-Ireland Congress, though the
proceedings generally were characterised by an earnest desire to
maintain first principles and to approach details in a practical
‘mood.

The temper of the gathering was good, and the despatch
of business, if slow, was thorough. But the discussions over
provincial and local matters gave birth to acrimony that
reflected adversely upon the demeanour of the delegates as 2
whole. It would seem to be a fact that minor disputes are
grasped with a tenacity sadly lacking in dealing with really
vital interests. It is the survival of such a spirit that is to be
dreaded, for the presence of such delegates hinders progress and
hampers a united advance.

The proceedings of a recent Connacht County Convention
were particularly unedifying in this respect. Recriminations,
innuendoes (we believe quite unjustified and unworthy) occupied
much of the delegates’ time. .

No doubt when laxity and disorganisation prevail trouble
is bound to arise at Conventions. They are the places to remedy
abuses and repair wrongs. But most irregularities are the out-
come of personal or district feuds (some of old standing), or the

41

mean desire of spiritless Gaels to secure unfair advantages and
unworthy honours.

Official incompetence or neglect is not a rare cause of
trouble either, and that co-operation between district officers and
club officials that proves the motive power of all great organi-
sations is not a feature of Gaelic athletic affairs, and its absence
1s sadly and frequently felt.

That re-election can be secured by delinguents and
offenders is the root of all the evil, and implies much that is
uncomplimentary to their Gaelic constituents.

Until care, and rigid care, is exercised in the selection of
officers and Councils—and the tendency now to restrict the
number on important Councils increases the necessity of such
caution—all the procedure concerned with our games will be
liable to failure, or at least reproach. It is a lack of the spirit
of true Gaelicism that breeds delinquents in the Council and
ind}iference in the ranks. The latter must be roused to a recog-
nition of their duty, security and danger, and the former must
be swept into official oblivion. A continuance of such tactless
methods, such sins of omission and commission, such ignorance
and disregard of fundamentals, could, if unchecked, destroy
ultimately more than all Zeal,” Devotion and Unselfishness
could create. Let us remember that the strength of the Associa-
tion lies in its well-organised supremacy, and that on it depends
the vital pulse-beat and perhaps the national fortunes and future
of our race.

COLLEGE ATHLETICS AND GAMES.

Qne of the most serious, as it is certainly one of the most
glaring of Irish anomalies, is the attitude adopted by colleges
towards national movements generally.  Sprung—principals
and pupils alike—from the people, the former set their faces
resolutely against the cultivation of the natural attri-
butes and popular traditions of the race, and so the latter, with
few exceptions, grow up alienated from their kin and divorced
by academic associations from the legitimate inheritances of
their people.

Wg have seen lament made that the native population were
from time to time (especially after the capitulation of Lime-
rick) deprived of their natural leaders; and excuse has been

42

derived from this fact for much of their subsequent misfortunes
and distractions. It is largely—indeed wholly—true; and, as
for centuries after, no serviceable opportunity was given them
of replacing their lost guides, the effect cannot be ignored nor
its evils exaggerated.

But under conditions less unfavourable for racial and social
reorganisation, what facilities or inducements were provided for
the creation of a new line of leaders—kindred in blood, united
in aims, and bound with the masses in sympathy and aspira-
tions? Did the schools endeavour to supply the need? Did
they educate to provide champions and captains for the dis-
ordered masses or organisers of art, industry, national pride
and mutual help amongst the artisans and peasantry? Ina
word, did they educate for the service of Ireland or for the ser-
vice of aliens? g

Can the answer be a favourable one? To-day can the
verdict be an approval of the actions of those to whom oppor
tunity fell.

Herein lies the genesis and explanation of the present colle-
giate stand against native heritages. Is it a justification ?

How long this boycott of native games can continue will
depend upon ourselves. Apart from sentiment for native games,
there are two arguments in their favour—one utilitarian and the
other ethical—that cannot be ignored. Firstly, the native games
are admittedly (as one should naturally expect) best fitted for
the physique and temperament of our people. A majority of
the pupils of our colleges have been reared amidst games such
as Canon Sheehan has so well depicted; and their yearning for
a continuance of such beneficial and innocent pursuits is surely
no more wicked than it is unnatural. But other games—foreign
and unnatural—are forced upon them with results as baneful
nationally as they are barren physically.

In addition to this practical aspect there remains the con-
sideration : is it wise or right to force students into an alien

arena which, in its original composition, represents all that was
inimical to the interests of their country and people? It can
have only one of two results—neither to be endured. The boy
turns gradually from all his first ideals, and renounces home,
love, patriotism, and race pride; or, bowing reluctantly to the
dictum of scholastic superiors, plays an unreal part—a dan-

43

gerous expedient for youth even in the seemingly minor matter
of school games and pastimes.

We will not further urge the unpatriotic attitude of the
colleges, nor discuss the oft-asserted, but wholly baseless, charge
of the general mismanagement of Gaelic games. Were it true,
it would be a reflection upon our own people; but still more, it
would be a bitter indictment of the colleges’ own action. To
rob the native games of the support and guidance of the best
educated and disciplined of our youth, and then charge themn
with lack of decorum and order, is much akin to the nature of
the English reproach of our ignorance and poverty—having first
banned the teachers and destroyed the commerce of our country.
The bona fides of such a class of critics cannot be admitted.

Will the pupils of our schools and colleges be permitted
(as they should be encouraged) to foster and practise Gaelic
games ? or must the hands of the authorities be forced once more
to cease poisoning Irish youth in the miasma of Anglicisation?
It is now over a quarter of a century since Dr. Croke expressed
his hope that the masters and pupils would no longer exclude
native games and pastimes from the playgrounds. They have
not travelled far towards the realisation of his wish.

A survey of the various competitions in operation will
reveal the scantiness of scholastic participation, though, of
course, the games—hurling especially—are practised in many
colleges. The Schools’ League in Dublin was killed by the

" passive but effective disapproval of the school authorities,

especially the headquarters of the I7ésk Christian Brothers. In
Munster, the College Competition is but a skeleton of what it
should be. The Louth League has suffered by the inexplicable
defection of its first winners. Such are the sinister evidences of
silent antagonism to native games.

But the pupils’ ideals and inclinations lie quite the other
way, as many letters from educational institutions prove. Here
then is a conflict between the natural advantage of Irish youth
and the interests of Irish games on the one hand, and the preju-
dice and “ respectability ” of Irish schools on the other.

Which will prevail ?

In any country but this, such a question would provoke
mirth. Here, and at present, it looks as if the inquiry could
only excite despondency, and the answer only provoke wrath.

4
ON FINANCE.

No matter what is under discussion, money, sooner or later,
proves a potent factor to be considered. In any project to pro-
mote our games, such as that which is now being made to
organise them in the Leinster Colleges, money is required, and,
as often as not, is unobtainable. There is no data immediately
available for ascertaining the gross annual receipts and expendi-
ture of all the various Gaelic Boards, but a glance at the sum-
mary of the Central and Provincial accounts will give the
amount passing through the hands of these bodies. That, in
the aggregate, the County and League Committees handle much
more is tolerably certain. Yet, how miserably small is the sum
devoted to effective organising, while none at all is set apart as
a reserve for special crises or opportunities.

This is certainly a grave mistake—one not at all defensible
by optimism or justified by experience. Many are only too pain-
fully aware of the inability of the Association to acquire
grounds, or to promote and bear the probable financial loss of a
great athletic carnival which might rivet the attention of the
athletic world once again upon our vitality as an athletic race.
That from end to end of Ireland no single playing arena is
under the control of the Association, is not creditable to the
wisdom or enterprise of the Councils, while the amount expended
year by year in rent would, if capitalised, prove. amazingly large.

We have recollections of several attempts made to acquire a -

Gaelic ground in the Metropolis, and on each occasion the Asso-
ciation, as a body, would have been unable to co-operate to any
useful extent. It is, furthermore, only prudence to provide
against unusual calls upon normal resources, lean years, or for
exceptional loss. The creation of a reserve should engage the
carnest attention of all Gaelic athletic bodies, but, more
especially, the Central and Provincial Councils, whose responsi-
bilities and possible demands may be greatest.

There are many desirable things that the possession of
available reserves would facilitate, but which the absence of
such places beyond reach. Of these, the importance of grounds
will generally be assigned first place, for much of the disorgani-
sation that occurs in many counties, and all the want of
adequate revenue, arise from lack of suitable and convenient

45

grqunds; while the extension of our games, and principally
Fheu promotion amongst the schools, will greatly depend upon
increase of playing facilities, and a consequent augmentation of
receipts.

This is a sordid topic, but a very important one; and, apart
_frorn all else, the regular dispersion of each year’s total income
isa spendthrift policy. It argues an indifference to permanent
interests and future probabilities.

3
AN ATHLETIC ASPIRATION

. Lugh Lamh Fada, we are told, instituted the Aonac of
Tall?enn in commemoration of Tailte—his mother—and queen
of his Apt:ople, Could not Gaeldom to-day re-organise such a
gathering in honour of Banba—the ever-living spiritual queen
of our race.  Surely, time never was more propitious, nor the
need greater, nor the prospective benefits more likely to be fully
reaped.  The Greeks, with the encouragement of friendly
powers, a}nd largely by the aid of a munificent countryman,
r_e-eftabhshed the Olympic games. While recognising the
limited fcsourcgs in every respect of their country, I think the
Greeks were guilty of a want of racial self-confidence in placing
the games under an International Committee, and of a sad indif-
fe'rence to the root significance of the event in making it
migratory. y

; Our position in the realm of athletics would equally well
entitle us to imitate the Greeks in their enterprise, if not in their
proc.edure. We are, thanks to the International Board, denied
Natnopal representation at the Olympic games, although in
athl:tlc_ material and achievement we can indisputably claim the
headship of European athletics. Our best games, hurling and
ham_ibal]; many of our most delightful events, the three jumps
(various methods), and all the weights, heavier and lighter than
the shot, are cut out from the Olympic programme. So that,
had‘we never an historic, we certainly have a very material in-
centive to revive our Olympiad, and safeguard such splendid
events.

It was pleasing to read of the couple of Provincial Ath-

~ letic Championships held this year, and we trust those in charge

will see to their continuance, and endeavour to differentiate them

46

as much as possible from stereotyped “sports”  We hope, in a
greater degree, that the National Athletic Championships will
be similarly conducted and better patronised in future. But no
multiplicity of such events could evoke the same enthusiasm
or yield the same national advantages that a real National Car-
nival could.

Half the programme has been already organized and re-
modelled by experience, for in the Oireachtas we have prac-
tically all the literary, musical, and most of the art elements
required. Can we not arrange the athletic, and, in conjunction
with the Oireachtas authorities and the Industrial Organisa-
tions, take a step towards the attainment of a great ambition?
No province, no element or sect or policy, no clash of contend-
ing interests, need enter into the project if loyally and
conscientiously pursued. It would cost money—a lot of
money—for, if attempted, it should be carried out in a befitting
manner. The joint annual expenditure of the three bodies
concerned—language, industrial and athletic—is great. The
resources of our kindred abroad—who might well make such an
occasion one for generous support and a home-coming—are
greater still.  In the athletic department, the entries reasonably
to be expected from the New World would alone make our
Aonac memorable.

Tt is too daring a scheme? Are Irish distractions and
lack-leal tendencies still too formidable for such a great re-
union? Could not art and manufactures be expected to lend a
hand in the realisation of the genius, versatility, and prowess
of our race? I cannot answer. One dare scarcely hope!

But T know it would be a great, a revivifying, and an in-
spiring event. It would arouse patriotism and race-pride where
they have long lain dormant. It would be the hosting, not of
a nation alone, but of a people scattered far, and fast losing
much of “all that brightens and adorns” It would herald the
rising once again of the star of a glorious race.  Can it be?
If it came to pass, one thinks he can hear the rattle of
Cuchullin’s chariot and the snorting of his famous steeds.
Yes; and the laughter of Finn and his men—and the quicker
heart-beat of a great future Ireland.

ATHLETIC LIFE AND EXPERIENCES OF MATT
McGRATH.

WRITTEN BY HIMSELF.

HE appreciation of the people of my native county for
T athletics makes the idea of writing about my work and
experiences alone excusable.

I was born and grew to healthy boyhood on a farm near the
town of Nenagh, Tipperary. Fireside tales, full of thrilling
memories of knightly deeds, fired my young heart with a desire
to be heard of in the world of athletics. I took early to the
pastime of putting the stone from the shoulder, and in the Irish
games of hurling and football I acquired the basis of the
strength which arrived with mature manhood. A boy’s daily

_ walk to and from school in the pure air accomplishes more for

the Irish athlete than all the drills, calisthenic courses, and
gvmnastic classes of other lands. Being fortunate in having
to walk six miles to and. from school, when about sixteen years
old, I unconsciously acquired that positive strength in body and
limbs so essential for all spheres of sport. The beautiful
scenery of Tipperary was never so appreciated as when I hunted
hares and rabbits on the mountains, when I frequently walked
over twenty-five miles in one day, besides jumping the fences
and farm boundaries. This, of course, was only when work on
my father's farm permitted, for I always assisted with the
labour on the land.

Tom Kiely, the world’s ex-All-round Champion, was then
in his prime, and I always wished to see him when he competed
in local meets. Jim Mitchel, the hammer-thrower (who left
Ireland with the Gaelic Invasion team in 1888) was making
records with various weights in America, and the delight which
his success evoked still further stimulated my athletic ambitions.
The fame of the Davin Brothers, the pioneers of progressive
Irish athletics, also engaged my admiration.

Ireland, though the home of athletics, is careless in develop-
ing men for individual competitions, and I had no inducement
to train except the inherent love of manly action and physical

48

beauty which is allied to Irish instinct. When a man of great
physical proportions visited my home, I followed his dep'artxng
form, secretly in silent admiration, believing that he, being of
massive mould, should be a marvellous man in action. My
early impressions are indelible, and I believe many an Irish
boy’s feelings are but vaguely outlined in my poorly-expressed
recollections. :

Before passing from my youthful scenes, I desire to make it
clear that it is by way of greeting to old friendsthat I write. No
man should boast of his strength, but he should regard physical
prowess as a God-sent gift. It is a duty to develop it, as would
a musician, a sculptor, a poet, or a painter, the peculiar talents
with which he has been endowed. There is another reason :
love of manly sport is a heritage of the Gael, and in out-door
athletics the Irishman has always shown remarkable ability.
From early life I was taught to believe that a man’s body was
the temple of his soul, and to have that temple as it should be,
its possessor should 'strive for its perfeation physically and
spiritually.

I left home when about eighteen years old, and, like most
young Irishmen, had difficulty in getting employment in New
York that would permit me time to practise athletics. 1 soon
found I was indeed far away from the scenes where farmers
never ploughed the oid pastures when the boys needed them for
hurling; yet I longed to be worthy of the race of that fine proto-
type of the Gael, Matt Donovan, the hammer-thrower of Knock-
na-Gow, drawn so brilliantly by the patriot, Kickham, and 1
practised a little in my scant leisure.

1 managed to keep in fair condition by nightly exercise in
a gymnasium.  The air, as is usual in all gymnasiums, was
stuffy and the space not large enough, so my exercise was
useless as a preparation for competition.

Opportunity offered when I joined the New York Police
Force. In 1906 I lived convenient to the Pastime Athletic
Club grounds. I joined this club, and there I first saw John
Flanagan training. 1 thought hammer-throwing was the pret-
tiest and most skilled action in athletics I have ever seen. The
foot-work in the seven-foot space reserved for the athlete, the
dexterous arm swing, speed and perfection in detail, are the fruit
of many years' experience linked to earnest effort, and backed

49

by the elemental essential--a powerful physique. Those
ignorant of athletics do not realise that intelligence is needed,
especially in weight-throwing. To acquire speed in restricted
space; the control, poise, and equilibrium of the body; judge
dls‘tancc, and learn how to make the sublime effort without
losing balance, or breaking rules, require insistent, intelligent
practice.

Besides hammer-throwing I also trained for the “ fifty-six,”
shot and discus. I had no instructor, and suffered much from
the strains caused by moulding my muscles to this form of
diversified training. I gave most attention to the hammer,
because it engages an athlete’s entire strength, is thrown further
than any other weight, and elicits most enthusiasm. My
greatest difficulty was to gain proper control, and even now my
technique is not yet perfected.

In 1906 T won the hammer in the Metropolitan Junior
Championships—a competition confined to athletes who never
won a championship. This was my only attempt in public that
year. I moved to Kensington (a South Brooklyn suburb) in
1907 ; purchased a set of weights, and began to train on a self-
outlined routine—making the mistakes peculiar to novices, but
Jim Mitchel was generous with advice and suggestion.

Following my victory in the Metropolitan Championships
with the hammer in 1907, I went to Kansas City, where I won
the Middle States “ Fifty-six” and Hammer Championships.
'l_‘he Junior National Championship at the Jamestown Exhibi-
tion was the next victory, which was soon followed by the Cana-
dian Championship at Montreal, where I broke the hammer
record with a throw of 173 ft. 7 ins. It had been held for ten
years by John Flanagan. I also won the “fifty-six” at the
same meet. That autumn the “fifty-six” for height record was
brokep by me at Madison Square Garden. In 1908, though
suffering from a dislocated knee, I got placed at the Olympic
Games in London with the hammer. If I never gained any-

tl¥ing by athletics than the glory of being a member of the
victorious American team at London in 1908 ,I would be satis-
fied. The beating administered to England there by America

USE ONLY PATERSON’S IRISH MATCHES.

50

and the Irishmen on the American team was a vindication of
what Irishmen could do when opportunity presented itself.
When I heard that the Ballsbridge meet, at which our boys
competed, was held under the rules of an athletic organisation
which has tried to thwart and obstruct the development of
national Irish athletics, I refused to compete, hurried from the
in-field, and left the grounds. ~The unnatural elimination in
the Dublin daily papers of everything vital to national progress
accounts for the silence regarding my refusal to compete at the
Dublin Police Sports Games. The London Sporisman, allud-
ing to the Dublin trouble, said that many of the American
athletes were policemen, forgetting that in Ireland policemen
are the people’s oppressors in the pay of their alien tyrant, while
American policemen are guardians of peace and property.

1 always had Ireland in my dreams from the time I left
until T returned. I believed that I would find great changes,
and that the old country would suffer when compared with

America. In no way was Ireland changed, but in beauty,-

warmth, and affection was as alluring as ever.

1 shall never forget the feelings with which I stepped into
the athletic field in Nenagh. I was within sight of the moun-
tains with which some of the greatest epics of our race are
associated. The soil was sacred with the shrines of saints and
the dust of my fathers raised within the shadows of those
Tipperary hills. 1 was at home, and if there was any chance
of my forgetting that fact, the wild cheers evoked by my
hammer throws were enough to convince me that the blood of
Tipperary flows as warm as ever. In and out of the athletic
field, I shall never forget the days spent when renewing boy-
hood friendships in my native parish.

Returning to New York, I won the Senior National Hammer
Championship, and also the Canadian Hammer, “ Fifty-six,”
and Discus Championships. I also won events in the weights in
great athletic reunions in Pittsburg, Chicago and Buffalo.

The Irish name has always been associated with feats of
strength, endurance and agility; disregard for danger, aggres-
siveness and pluck. Irish influence in amateur athletics has
placed America in the first rank. England is a poor loser.
She is sore over the triumphs of the American team. England
is paying the price that is the inevitable fate of nations that lust

51

for power and gold. She may as well take her defeats as grace-
fully as possibly, and remember that Irish brawn will not
strengthen her weakness. Her colonies are gradually drifting
from Saxon traditions, and all the Irish record-holders in
America were developed by Gaelic pastimes.

Athletics are splendid exercises for young men, but great
care should be taken in selecting certain events. Not every
man is endowed with strength to become a hammer-thrower,
but running and jumping are sports in which any man may hope
for success, and the lighter built the better.

Ireland’s athletes are not a great aid to their native land,
for they have to seek fortune’s favours in other countries. But
let every young Irishman develop his strength with the hope
that he may one day use it towards the restoration of his country

as a nation among the nations of the earth.
\

MATT MCGRATH.
[Since the foregoing was written Matt has raised another
‘World’s Record—viz., throwing 561b. for height, as he succeeded
in reaching the remarkable altitude of 16 feet 6} inches.—ED.]

THE LIMERICK COMMERCIAL FOOTBALL CLUB.
ITS HISTORY.

HE origin of this old Club dates back to the early eighties.
When the G.A.A. was unknown to our people the
Limerick Commercials, under D. H. Ryan and the late Tom
O'Connor, were trying hard to spread the light. In 1883 they
played their first challenge match against the Limerick Butchers,
in the old Ballinacurra Race Course, kindly given by Mr.
Hogan, who, it may be said, was always willing to do a favour
to the Gaelic Association.

g The game ended in a win for the Commercials.  Their
opponents were a powerful body of men, but not so expert, and
had to go under.

This victory fired the players so much that they became
enthusiastic, and worked like men to make the game a success.
In 84 they played the next big match against Meelick F.C., and
had an casy win over the giants by the Shannon. Soon'after
they met St. Patricks, under Captain Gough—a real Gael. The
match was played in the Canal fields; and again they had a well-
earned victory. Up to this time the Club was more or less a
private cne, being mainly composed of the young men drawn
from the drapery warehouses, and a few other shop assistants.

The late Tom O’Connor was Captain, and D. H. Ryan (of
Thurles), was Hon. Sec. Much to the credit of D. H,, he saw
there was a great future before the G.A.A. and the Commercials.
He summoned a special general meeting of his Club at
McNamara's Boot Warehouse, William Street, for the purpose
of extending operations, and, through his foresight, the move
was successful.  All the commercial athletic young men of the
city were invited to become members, and immediately its ranks
were filled up by the best athletes of the South.

At the first subsequent meeting of the Committee, Dan
Ryan well said: “I have now an army of athletes that would
do honour to any country, and the G.A.A. must go ahead.”
By this time D. H. was Captain.
