At this particular time, Limerick was a very strong Rugby
centre, and this was looked upon as very strong language.
However, he realised the dream of his life, and succeeded.
With a strong team, he arranged matches with St. Michaels, St.
Johns, and St. Patricks, all of which met defeat. He then fixed
up matches with every team in the county, winning as he went
along. In the winter of ’85 and ’86, clubs had sprung up every-
where.  Tipperary and Cork were very active, and doing well.
A match was arranged on Good Friday with the “Lees,” and,
after a stubborn contest, the Commercials won with a good
margin. Immediately after, they travelled to Kilrush by
special steamer, and defeated the home team. The next big
fixture was Bansha, where they played two matches, against
“Bansha” and the Tipperary “Rosannas,” again coming out
victors.  These were two slashing teams.

On this occasion the first special train over the Waterford
and Limerick Railway was run for Gaelic matches, and had to
be guaranteed, on behalf of the Club, by D. H. Ryan and
Maurice Fitzgibbon.

Later on in '86 they travelled to the Limerick Junction to
meet the famous Bohercrowe team, and defeated them after a
fast match. Towards the end of this season they again
defeated the “Lees” at Cork, and “ Kilmacduane ” at Kilrush,
besides several teams through the neighbouring counties.

In this year, '86, was held the big Gaelic sports at Limerick,
mainly promoted by the Commercials, in which they took a
prominent part, winning the lion’s share of the prizes.

Now comes the famous year of 87.  Just as the Champion-
ships were announced, they met the first disappointment in the
loss of their Captain, D. H. Ryan, whose commercial pursuits
called him to Dublin.  This was a severe blow, as nobody was
left fit to take his place. He was a born organiser. However,
the best had to be done, and, strange to say, the choice fell to a
non-player, poor Pat Treacy, long since gone to his reward. It
may not be generally krown to-day that Limerick had then two
County Boards. The new or rival one was led by the famous
Father Sheehy, against the Old Board, as they were then called.
They were fairly evenly balanced, and struggled hard for
supremacy and recognition at headquarters. The Commercials,
even at the loss of members, stood by the Old Board, who were

54

the founders, believing they were right, in the interest of the
G.A.A. The Father Sheehy Board carried away many, of the
leading clubs, and they played out for the Championships.

Under the Old Board, the Commercials won out their
Championships in easy fashion, and were partnered by the
“South Liberties ” in hurling. Now stood two rival champions,
and no decision from the Central Council of which was the
legally constituted one. At last, it was decided that both cham-
pions should play off, and the winners be recognised.

Both rival Boards were called together, and the venue was
fixed for Croom, with the Central Council officiating. Rela-
tions were very strained, and the contest was expected to be
very hot on both sides. The eyes of all Munster were fixed on
the Limerick Gaels—in fact, all Gaelic Ireland was interested.

The Commercials and South Liberties decided to travel by
rail, and again it came to their turn to get the first special train
for the G.A.A. over the G.S. and W. Railway. After great pres-
sure, and a guarantee, they succeeded, but on this occasion it
took six trains to accommodate the living freight for Croom,
so high did feeling run, and so strong was the Association.
These were all a Commercial and Liberty following, as the
others travelled by coaches.

After a vigorous match of fast, rough play, the Commerciais
won easily from St. Michaels with a big margin; and, after a
desperate struggle of fast, furious play, the South Liberties de-
feated Murroe, thus putting the Old Co. Board in the ascendant.
This victory had a still greater effect; it united the divided
Gaels, and made them brothers, as they should ever be.

After this the Commercials won through. Munster, and
afterwards met Leinster. First they met Meath in Elm Park,
Dublin, and, after a nice, scientific game, they won readily.
Again they were required to travel to Dublin to play Kilkenny
(Kilmacow), which, after a desperate struggle, ended in a draw
(1 goal 10 points each). The day blew a storm, and scientific
play was impossible.

Three weeks later they were again asked to try conclusions
with Kilmacow, and Bansha was fixed as the venue. In bright
sunshine, in a beautifully equipped meadow field, right in front
of the Glen of Aherlow, both teams toed the line. For a moment
Kilmacow seemed to be winning, but, once the Commercials

55

settled down, there was no stopping them, and their ground-
work and rushes carried everything before them, so the “brave
rolling blades” had to submit to defeat by a large margin.

This was the Semi-Final, and they had yet to meet Louth,
Dundalk, Young Irelanders. Dublin was once more fixed as
the meeting ground. This was a wonderfully fast game,
scientific and clever, but the “ old reliables” (as they were then
called) stayed out better, and won a sensational match with
something to spare. They received congratulations on all sides,
and from all counties, for the manly way in which they fought
out the Championships, and won, with undisputed merit, the
first and one of the greatest Championships of the G.A.A.

During all these matches the Commercials never received
a penny towards their expenses, although they were promised
such on each journey to Dublin. Money poured in at every gate
like rain, but the governing body of the time gave no considera-
tion to the teams—a most unjust and iniquitous procedure.

Is there any other club in Ireland who has done so much for
the G.A.A, and met such bad treatment at the hands of the
Central Council, only after 23 years to come in for any recogni-
tion, or get their medals ?

It may be stated here that the club held an unbeaten record
up to 18go.

The team was made up of business men, and it suffered
each year from wastage, some going to Dublin, Cork, and
Anmerica; but they had a coming lot, always ready to recruit
from, and by this means kept up the steam. Still the actions of
the powers that were at this time damped the ardour of one of
the most spirited and unselfish bodies of Gaels in the country.

To the Limerick Commercials, in a great measure, was due
the conversion of a whole city from imported games. Had mat-

ters gone on as they should with the G.A.A. of the day, precau-

tions against the Anglicisation of the Gael in 1910 would not
be needed. But the big hearts of the men who were playing
the game and bearing the expense grew tired. Their enthusiasm,
for want of the recognition that was due to it, began to wane as
years went by, and promises remained unfulfilled, so that the
Gaelic fire which then burned so fiercely in Limerick City and
County sank to but the embers of its former self. Still the old
hands kept fanning the flame; and—as the young blood came

56

along—by example and encouragement, aroused again such
energy and enthusiasm that, in the year 1896, another genera-
tion annexed that coveted title—the Gaelic Football Champion-
ship of their country. )

For almost 26 years the Limerick Commercials have been
champions of Limerick County, and, during all these years, were
considered one of the best individual teams in the country.
Although taking football easy at present, before very long they
will again be heard of, and, I am sure, be as formidable as when
Din Corbett led them in 1887 and subsequent years. Their
great combination play from 1806 to 1905 will ever be remem-
bered.

Other great teams have come for a short spell, and have
have had bright careers, but they lapsed, and have gone out of
existence in a few short years, but the Limerick Commercials
were always in the front rank, and were never lost to the
G.A.A. for one day.

Theirs was a hard task to accomplish in the face of great
opposition, local influences, migration and emigratign, whic}? are
prevalent in this district to a pitiful degree. but still the Lime-
rick Commercials kept the flag of the G.A.A. flying topmost for
over six and twenty years.

So, after twenty-three years, thanks to the energy of the
Provincial Council and the generosity of the big-hearted Gaels
who gave authority at the last G.A.A. Congress, the champions
of ’87 are to come into possession of some fitting token of their
prowess, which may remind them of the strenuous events in t.he
early building up of that mighty athletic institution in which
they took such a manly part. Let their story be recounted for
many years to come around happy firesides in Erin, or wherever
fortune has scattered their compeers throughout the world.

P. ] CORBETT.

57

HANDBALL: A PLEA.

ERMIT me to call the attention of the Gaels of Ireland

to a centuries old, but somewhat neglected, national game

in Ireland. The Irish and American-Irish are the only handball

players to be found the world over. It has suffered nothing in

either merit or distinctiveness because it has been untouched by
the Saxon.

Like all other games, it has gone through transition stages
since our grandfathers played with sheep-skin balls.  Then
every parish had its ball-alley, and every ball-alley its cham-
pions and enthusiastic supporters. To-day we find the game in
a very disorganised state, indeed.

True, ball courts are to be found in every county, but there
is no uniformity of structure, nor unanimity as to the kind of
handball to be used.

It is the duty of the Gaelic Athletic Association to foster
handball as much as hurling or football. What we want most,
I think, is guidance and aid in the means of putting the game
on a good sound national basis. To do so the Council must
decide what kind of handball is to be regarded as the stan-
dard. On that depends the formation of ball courts and style
of play adopted. ¥

The present state of affairs is simply this: professionals
play with hard or solid balls in enclosed courts. Amateurs
play with rubber balls in all kinds of courts, and even at gable
ends. Some open courts have side walls to ace line and some
to over line. The open court has much to recommend it as a
national structure. It is universal, except in cities, and it
affords ample space at back for spectators’ gallery, and spec-
tators must be considered. There is, I think, still existing
some copies of the old and unrevised handball rules which mav
be useful as a reference in re-establishing this grand old Gaelic
pastime—a pastime which should be dear to the heart of every
Irishman who realises that he has a country to love and a
national game to revive and foster.

P. J. KEIGHRON, Monasterborce.

58

THE NEW RULES AND THINGS IN GENERAL.

WILL the new rules make our games more ;_)opular? I
think they will. The alteration in the scoring area may
be said to be the most drastic, and many members of the Asso-
ciation have viewed it with alarm. I will commence by saying
that I never could sum up sufficient courage to defend the old
scoring area when heckled on the point, with its forty-five feet
in width and unlimited height to score a point. Any defence
that could be made was in the case of hurling, lest any altera-
tion should tend to diminish, if not altogether do away with,
“ whip play,” which unquestionably is one of the finest charac-
teristics of the game. To my mind, at all events, hurling easily
takes first place amongst all the games played in Ireland, ar}d
perhaps outside Ireland. There is a dash and go about it all its
own. Itisas “Celt” has aptly said: “the nearest approach to
warfare consistent with peace,” and therefore any alteration in
the rules governing it should be carefully thought out. This
the Sub-Committee appointed by the County Dublin Committee
did; it was not without diffidence some of us agreed to any
tampering with the scoring area, principally because we were
afraid it may be the means of dispensing with the “ whip play ”
already referred to. Various suggestions were made, 2nd a
compromise was arrived at in the shape of the present scoring
space. Has the alteration injured the game. I think not.
True, it has diminished the strong, forceful play of the for-
wards, but there is more brainy work than was formerly the
case, and a lesser number of wild shots resulting in “ overs,” and
as a direct consequence it has increased the number of minutes
in which play is acfually in progress. This latter is very impor-
tant, considering that rarely, if ever, any extra time is played.
And again, it gives the goal-man the opportunity of saving
scores, because, unlike olden times, the goals played for now
are very much greater and the points infinitely less. The rule
allowing only ten men to line up in the middle of the field isalso
a useful one, inasmuch as it prevents the possibility of any team
securing an easy score without any play in the first stages of
the game, and for which the team was not value. The nets,

59

too, are a decided advantage, so much so that their adoption
needs no defence, and it is satisfactory to recall that their use-
fulness was, as far as I know, first pointed out in the pages of
the ANNUAL published a few years ago.

As regards football, the altered scoring area has done much
to improve the footwork, and although catching and punting
may be said to be two fine characteristics of the game, yet a good
deal could be said against the high points as a result of those
characteristics. I know, of course, many arguments could be
employed against my theory, but yet I imagine this change in
conjunction with some others are beneficial to the pastime. The
changes in the rules have made much difference to many players,
but more particularly to old players who have not been readily
able to adapt themselves to the new conditions—-in fact it can
be said that many Inter-County teams have not so far been able
to strictly conform to the new rules, and it is on behalf of such
players and teams I would enter a plea to all officials. The
rules as now promulgated impose a very considerable restraint
on players as compared with former times, and, if interpreted in
the strict letter of the law, will spoil many a good game. In
hurling, for instance, it is not to my mind desirable that in puck-
ing in the ball from the side line a man should be forced to
make a perceptible halt before striking, provided always that
he merely walks to where the linesman places the ball.  This
rule was framed with the view to preventing players securing
a decided advantage by running to a ball, and I hold if a player
walks to it he secures no more advantage than if the ball were
actually placed at his feet, and hence there is no necessity for
stopping play and awarding the other side a free puck. There
is then the very serious matter of the parallelogram and the
grave duty it imposes on goal umpires, and it seems to me
when there is any element of doubt it should be given to the
scoring team in all cases. With some teams—notably Cork and
Kilkenny in hurling, and Kerry and Louth in football—the
rules applied in the strictest sense produce the very best results
both in the play itself and in the pleasure afforded the spec-
tators; but will anyone seriously doubt that if the same strict-
ness were applied to other teams many matches would not be
utterly spoiled? It is therefore most desirable that Referees
Associations should be formed in every county, in which every

6o

man securing membership—and only members of the Referees’
Associations should be allowed to officiate even in friendly
matches—would not in the slightest degree vary the interpreta-
tion placed on the rules by the majority of his colleagues. Many

men are disposed, and rightly so, to use their discretion, for an

experienced man can see after a few minutes’ play whether or
not teams are constantly forced to adhere to the strict letter of
the law, or whether a certain amount of latitude is allowed
them in their home matches. Lest I should be misunderstood,
I may say that it is only in the absence of the Referees’ Associa-
tion I am in favour of discretion being applied; and, of course,
when it is not inconsistent with the rules and combined with
firmness when firmness is needed.

The foregoing, although of sufficient importance to be dealt
with in the ANNUAL, are merely matters of a domestic nature,
and what is therefore of greater importance to us is : how do we
stand in regard to rival organisations? Few people will deny
we have knocked a good shake out of the Rugby and Soccer,
and with the advent of hurling and football in the higher schools
and colleges, we can mark a still greater advance which time
alone will reveal in all its fulness ; but how do we stand in regard
to athletics and cycling? True, we have a greater number of
meetings each year than the alien bodies can lay claim' to in
five or more years, and we have a greater number of athletes
and cyclists, but this is not sufficient so long as these bodies
can proclaim to the world that they represent Ireland, and at
the same time receive such a severe drubbing as the so-called
Irish team received this year from Scotland. It may be said :
why does not the G.A.A. promote international contests? The
answer is: our exclusive rules block the way so long as our
enemies are allowed to exist. Foreign countries, where the
reasons for our exclusive rules are not understood, cannot be
expected to sympathise with us nor render any assistance, more
particularly since our rivals were allowed, in the early days of
G.A.A, to regulate agreements as their own right with other
countries, whilst the members of our Association were content to
have an understanding on some insignificant points with the
TIA.AA. and LCA. The Athletic Associations of other
countries see no reason to break with their old-time allies, and
hence our isolation. But is there no remedy? There is; it is

61

at hand, and should be availed of. The remedy is to smash the
rival organisations root and branch. Can we do it? We can
if the business be approached in a determined manner. We
have been sparring now for some years, and it is nearly time we
came to hard blows. This is not the place to go into minute
details as to how that end may be accomplished ; as, apart from
the question of tactics, it would take up too much space, but one
of the remedies may be referred to in brief. It is well known
that at the present moment the Athletic Council holds hundreds
of applications from members of the other bodies for reinstate-
ment to the National Association, and that amongst the appli-
cants aré numerous champions and ex-champions; men who
must be relied on to make up international teams.” Why not,
under certain conditions, give these men a trial, or, in other
words, reinstate them, or at all events reinstate such of them as
the Council have sufficient faith in to adhere to their pledges?
There is no crime in taking into the fold men who have seen the
error of their ways, whether from a purely national standpoint
or whether from the selfish standpoint of being enabled to
compete with the alien Association. Through this medium
I am convinced we could secure at least seventy-five
per cent. of the alien bodies membership, and thus,
while Associations without members may be objects to
commiserate with, théy could scarcely be considered potent
for good or evil. With the decks cleared of such bodies
which have never reflected any credit on Ireland, and have
always been prepared to relegate her to a position of obscurity
as was instanced at the last Olympic games, the G.A.A. could
go to the representatives of foreign countries, and, without fear
of refusal, claim for Ireland her rightful place amongst the
athletic nations of the world. This is one of my ideas to effect
our athletic freedom; if it does not commend itself to others,
let us have their schemes, for all will admit it does not brook
delay. There can be no compromise, and there certainly can-
not be any peace, until some Association or Associations go
under, and that Association cannot be the G.A.A.

M. F. CrowE.

THE FIRST HURLING CHAMPIONS.

HURLES, representing Tipperary, won the first All-Ire-
land Hurling Championship of 1887. In their struggle
to win the county preliminaries Thurles defeated Two-Mile-
Borris at Holycross. Then, as now, Borris was a great team.
The match was very exciting, and was well contested. The
next game was played at what was known as the “Store Jug
Field,” Thurles, from which Borrisoleigh retired defeated. At
the third of the County matches the pick of North Tipperary
lined out against Thurles at Borrisoleigh. ~ After a rattling
good game Thurles won the County final. At Nenagh, Clare
was defeated by 1 goal 7 points to 2 points, and the first Mun-
ster Championship was Tipperary’s. The Leinster representa-
tives, Kilkenny, were next vanquished—the following being
from a contemporary report of the match : — The Inter-County
Hurling tie between Kilkenny and Tipperary was played off
to-day, Thursday, at Urlingford.  Thurles represented Tip-
perary, and Urlingford Kilkenny. The weather in the morning
showed very bad, as heavy showers fell. Before noon the rain,
however, ceased, and during the time of play not a drop
descended. Precisely at two o'clock the play commenced, and
in the first five minutes Thurles placed a goal to its credit. At
the end of full time the following extraordinary result was
announced : —For Thurles, 4 goals 6 points, and one forfeit
point to nil for Tullaroan. A large crowd of people was on the
field, and the order maintained was excellent.” The All-Ire-
land final was played at Birr on Easter Sunday, March, 1888.
The rival team was Galway, and after a game largely in Gal-
way’s favour, Thurles, by desperate and characteristic play in
the concluding minutes, retrieved their fortunes, and won by a
goal scored in the last second almost.

The following are the names of the winners in the final game,
but, of course, the list does not comprise all who helped in pre-
ceding matches to win first premier honours for Tipperary : —

matches, and taken twenty-three years after, is a unique testi-
mony to the physique of the men and the merits of our national

NORTH LOUTH SCHOOLS LEAGUE.

HE promotion of the work and influence of the G.A.A. in
T the schools of the county formed the subject of a motion
at the 190g Co. Louth Convention, and in a very short time after
steps were taken to give practical effect to the intention in North
Louth. The schools responded well, and in the spring of that
year we had nine clubs actively competing for the honour of
being first school champion.

From the very inauguration the work of the promoters was
made easy, and a cup was offered for the competition by Mr.

_ S. H. Moynagh, a local gentleman who took a very active interest
in the subsequent progress of the League. St. Mary's College,
the first champions, were prominent from the start, and though
they met with stout opposition from teams representing the
Christian Brothers and De La Salles, still they managed to
avoid a single defeat. The games, which were practically all
played on week days, generally attracted iarge crowds, and in
every respect the first season gave evidence of a very successful
future for the competition among the boys.  The standard of
play, too, shown by most of the teams was astonishingly good,
and reflected in a marked degree the excellence which is
generally evident among Louth players of all ranks.

It is casily inferring that the work of the first season was
most encouraging and far better prospects seemed in store, for
at the conclusion the Committee were presented with a further
cup for competition among the junior section of each of the
schools. Mr. Edward Goodman, an old Gael, was the donor.
However, the Committee were not long destined to pursue their
good work uninterruptedly, for, just as the games of the 1910
season were about to start, intimation was received that St.
Mary’s College intended withdrawing from the competition.
No reason was then assigned for the astounding action of the
College, and no reason has since been adduced by the
Superiors for their decision at such a critical period. In any
case, the resumption was delayed for a long time, and subse-
quently other causes intervened to retard the good work of the

65

previour year. At the moment of writing it looks as if the
same success would not reward the promoters as last year. Still
the originators of the League look to the future confidently.
The game will continue to flourish among the school children
of North Louth.

1t should be stated that, in the working of this competition,
the Christian Brothers have given their whole-hearted support
to the Committee, while the De La Salle Brothers have also
responded in the same spirit.  The National Schools, under the
control of the Dominican Fathers at Dromiskeen, have always
been ready to take their place in the ranks of the G.A.A, and it
only requires a little effort to persuade other schools in the
vicinity of Dundalk to be up and doing.

South Louth has been singularly inactive, and, with the
exception of the Drogheda Christian Brothers, none of the other
schools have shown a disposition to imitate the action of the
northern section, but here again much good work can be done
by a few earnest Gaels, and, if practical steps were taken, there
is material enough in the southern end of the county for a
splendid competition among the schoolboys. The County Com-
mitte will only too readily respond to any application that may
be made to stimulate interest in the Association, and no better
means can be found than by encouraging the sport in the
schools. Were it not for the encouragement given to foreign
games in most of the colleges, and many of the ordinary schools
of the country, the number of adherents of imported pastimes
in Ireland would be very few indeed, and when National
pastimes gain more popularity among the rising generation, the
future of the Association will be assured.

A PROPOSED GAELIC ATHLETIC WEEKLY
JOURNAL.

EEING that the Press of Ireland, as a whole, has not
appreciably improved in its attitude towards National
athletics, the question of providing a remedy for existing re-
quirements is one that calls for our serious consideration. Our
Metropolitan papers continue to treat us with as much nig-
gardliness as they possibly can. The minimum of space is
doled out to us, and, generally speaking, the matter which
they publish appertaining to G.A.A. doings is infinitely
inferior, in quality as well as quantity, to that provided con-
cerning foreign branches of sport. ~We might have expected
that the consciousness of a patriotic duty towards a National
movement like ours should have produced some change in our
National dailies at any rate, but apparently they are not
troubled with any such consciousness. Their commercial
instinct is likewise blind to the financial gain which, it seems
reasonable to expect, would accrue to them if they catered for
the National games in such a manner as would guarantee the
support of the thousands throughout the country who are in-
terested in our games.

In the Provincial Press the position is somewhat more
favourable. Some localities are fairly well catered for, and
much valuable assistance is thus rendered to the progress of our
games.  Such papers will naturally confine their attention to
recording the events which occur in their own special districts;
but even then their efforts will be productive of much good if
they are continued generously and consistently from week to
week, and are conducted in a spirit of purity and progressive-
ness.

‘While we must strain every effort to prevail upon the metro-
politan and provincial papers to recognise our claims for fuller
recognition of our games in their columns, we can do something
ourselves to provide a journal which would devote itself solely
to the interests of the G.A.A. A project for the establishment
of an All-Ireland G.A.A. Journal is at present before the

67

country, and has received the sanction and approval of the
All-Ireland Congress, the Councils, many County Committees,
and a large number of Gaels throughout the country. Briefly.
the proposal is to form a Gaelic Company for the promotion
of a weekly journal which would contain reports and criticisms
of all matches played under the G.A.A. and also of meetings
of G.A.A. Councils, Committees, etc. ~Items of special interest
to the movement would be included, and every effort would be
made to provide an attractive and profitable budget of news.
For the promotion of such a paper, a capital of £500 is re-
quired.  Obviously, this would not involve the acquisition of
a printing plant, as it is proposed that the printing of the paper
should be undertaken under contract by a printing firm, just
as happens in the case of many of the weekly journals in the
country.  Of the required capital, promises to the extent of
zbout £160 have already been received. ~The amount required
is not large, considering the extent and influence of our Asso-
ciation. Yet, the appeal is being answered so slowly that one
is inclined to feel that Gaels are not sufficiently alive to the
necessity that exists for such a paper, and to the duty that
rests upon them to do all in their power to render the proposal
an actuality.

1t should scarcely be necessary to outline what advantages
would accrue if a ‘paper such as that proposed were in
existence. In the first place, it would be a central journal, in
which matches played in all parts of the country would be
reported, and it would thus afford a true impression of the
strength of our forces, and of the progress being made in the
different centres. At present we are fearfully ignorant as to
how our brother Gaels are faring outside our own district. We
do not know what are the prominent teams, and how the local
championships and competitions are progressing. We would
remain in continual and helpless ignorance were it not for the
appearance of the G.A. ANNUAL, but this, alas, excellent as
it is, is only an annual production. The weekly journal would
give full and trustworthy reports of the meetings of our Coun-
cils, and the decisions would be read with eagerness all
through the country. It would veritably be a comprehensive
record of the week’s activity in Gaeldom, and the sphere of its
influence will comprise the entire country. Weekly sketches of

68

players and clubs, special articles concerning the movement by
prominent men in the Association, historical articles, leading
articles on important events, etc.—these would all have the
effect of creating greater interest in the Association, of awaken-
ing in us a keener appreciation of our duties and responsibilities,
and of making us more thoroughly alive to the principles under-
lying our movement. The benefits which it would confer on
our athletics would be more considerable still. The number of
athletic meetings held under G.A.A. laws is now very large,
and to follow the performances of our athletes is a difficult
matter. In addition to reporting all such meetings, the weekly
paper could effect great improvement in the different branches
of sport, by placing at the disposal of budding athletes,
articles containing the results of the varied experience of our
champions and successful performers. The journal would
afford valuable assistance in the work of organising centres in
which the Association is weak, and of redressing abuses and
irregularities which may arise here and there. Its columns
would provide a common ground on which Gaels in all parts
can interchange opinions on their difficulties, and on which they
can compare and adjust differences in procedure and in inter-
pretations of rules, etc.

These are but a few of the advantages which must follow
from the establishment of an All-Ireland Journal devoting
itself to the interests of the G.A.A.  The following are the

IRISH COLLEGES AND NATIONAL PASTIMES.

S our Association continues year by year to increase in
strength and importance, it is satisfactory to observe
{hat, concurrently with its growth and expansion, it is steadily
becoming more perfect in organisation. - Throughout the
general body of Gaels, and amongst the members of our Com-
mittees and Councils, there is exhibited a greater desire to act
according to the principles and the spirit of the Association.
The altered constitution of the Central Council has rendered
that body of more manageable dimensians, and has also in-
creased its authority and its responsibility. The change in the
scoring area in Hurling and Football has tended in the direc-
tion of improved and more scientific play, while it has resulted
in the retention of interest in our contests up to the “final
whistle,” by keeping the issue open to the very end. In ath-
letics, the Assodatign has become, beyond question, the
predominant athletic organisation in the country, and the
Athletic Council has succeeded, by capable administration, in
stamping out abuses and irregularities by which the existence
of pure sport in Ireland was long threatened.  All these evi-
dences of progress are highly satisfactory, but a matter for
greater congratulation, perhaps, is the change which is taking
place in the attitude of the Colleges towards our National
pastimes.

Ten years ago, the Colleges which afforded their students
an opportunity for acquiring a knowledge of their native lan-
guage were few in number, and fewer still were those which
allowed their students to practise, even in an informal way,
their native games. But, thank God, the influence of the Irish
Revival Movement has extended to all parts of the country,
and to all phases of Irish life.  The fight for the recognition
of the language as an essential subject in the education of a
cultured Irishman has terminated in a manner fully conform-
able to the demands of the nation. In regard to the language,
the National University has declared for the only policy which
was consistent with its title and its position; and to-day we find

the claims of Irish fully recognised in all our Catholic Col-
leges at least, and, in addition, the language is taught by them
with enthusiasm and efficiency.

The Colleges have, however, exhibited a slow and unwilling
disposition to adopt our native pastimes.  But, even in this
department, considerable progress must be admitted, when it is
borne in mind that, not many years ago, there was scarcely a
College, outside the Dominican College in Newbridge and a
few others, in which the National games were officially recog-
nised. The boycott of our National games in the Colleges has,
undoubtedly, affected the progress of our organisation to a
serious extent. The boys attending Colleges are largely
drawn from country districts, and many of them had been
accustomed to play their country’s games before they were sent
to College. Having arrived at College, they were compelled
to play a new set of games—those of the foreigner. Native
games were regarded as degrading and inferior, and, therefore,
as things to be avoided.  On the other hand, every facility was
afforded whereby the boys would become proficient in the games
of the foreigner. The special hours set apart for recreation
were devoted to the practice of these games, under the super-
vision and patronage of the superiors and masters; the services
of professional experts were very often requisitioned to perfect
the efforts of the boys; and, more important still, the chief
events of College life were matches in foreign games with out-
side teams and Colleges. The needs of “discipline,” it was
also urged, required that boys should participate in the same
field games during times of recreation, and those games must,
of course, be the foreign games. All these factors combined to
produce in the boys, during their formative years, a prejudice
in favour of the carefully fostered alien games, on the one
hand, and a strong_antipathy to the neglected and despised
National games on the other. The former arose naturally
from their constant opportunities for practising the games, from
the glamour with which these games were surrounded, and also
from the fact that these were the games represented as played
by the respectable classes outside; while the antipathy was
begotten of the impression persistently forced upon them that
the native games were the unskilled pastimes of a savage and
uncontrollable peasantry. Indeed! Vet it is that same

72

peasantry that has kept alive our National language, and to
them we turn now gratefully to gain back that priceless treasure
of our race which, but for them, would long since have become
extinct! It is the peasantry that sends forth fresh and
forceful intellects to adorn our professions! It is the peasantry
that forms the backbone of all our National movements! And
it is the same peasantry, likewise that preserves the physical
qualities of the race, and sends forth men who, in athletic
prowess, surpass the products of the whole world—worthy suc-
cessors of the Fianna! Who, then, dares to brand as inferior
the inherited manly pastimes in which our peasantry have exer-
cised for ages, and retained the physical pre-eminence of the
race ?

Admitting that the authorities in our Colleges were not
affected by any patriotic sentiments towards our games, some
circumstances might be adduced as a plea for their predilection
for foreign games. The foreign games have enjoyed a hold
on the Colleges, which dates beyond the period of the first
organised efforts to promote native games. Those in authority
in the Colleges were themselves the product of the College
system, having, in their own College days, been infected with
the prejudice for foreign games. In addition, the Colleges are
an imitative lot as a rule, and the practices of the large Col-
leges are faithfully followed by those rivalling their greatness
and their pretensions, right down the scale. To adopt native
games meant a mighty break with tradition, and also involved,
unfortunately, isolation from the other Colleges to a large
extent.  All the Colleges played the foreign games, and it was,
consequently, easy to secure inter-College fixtures in those
games. . There were also Inter-College Cup Competitions
which were open to them. The Colleges playing Gaelic games
were few in number, and were situated widely apart. To these,
the few Inter-College matches which were possible involved
much travelling and trouble, and College competitions did not
exist, nor were the external competitions suitable to the condi-
tions of the Colleges. In many Colleges, the authorities assert
that the selection of games is left in the hands of the boys. But,
how would the weight of College traditions and the desire to
conform to the wishes of those in charge incline them? Almost
invariably, the boys have chosen the games which brought them

73

the most outings and greatest freedom-—and small blame to
them for it. These are facts we must face, and, if we wish to
win over the Colleges to our side, we must exert ourselves to
place our games on an equal footing with the pampered products
of Anglicisation.

To Munster belongs the credit of having been the first pro-
vince to make effectual endeavours to secure for our games their
rightful place in the esteem of College boys. For a few years
now, Provincial College Championships have been successfully
carried out in that province. ~The competitions comprise Junior
and Senior Championships in both Hurling and Football, and
all are well supported. In the county of Cork alone, eleven
Colleges are this year amongst the competitors for the Cham-
pionships—some of them having entered as many as four
teams.  Truly, this is a record of which the province might well
be proud, and which might with advantage be emulated in a
small way by the other provinces! The strongholds of
Anglicisation have been attacked and conquered; and the Col-
lege traditions with regard to foreign games have been
grappled with dnd strangled. In a few large Colleges, the
foreign flag still flies rampant (more shame to them!) and these
remain the last footholds of Anglicising games in Munster.
Let us fervently hope that the brilliant examples of the other
Colleges, and the magnificent success of the College Cham-
pionships, will effect the¢ immediate capitulation of those still
outside the fold. The revival influence has pervaded these
Colleges in other directions, and with confidence we look to
them to let their patriotic instincts get the upper hand. No
longer, then, shall they handicap their charges by foisting upon
them games which are now played only by the garrison element
in that province.

The Colleges of Leinster and the rest of Ireland have long
withstood all efforts to bring them into line. Concurrently with
the initial endeavours in Munster, the Leinster Colleges were
approached, but without success.  Further efforts made subse-
quently were also ineffectual.  But steadily and quietly the
games were forcing their way into the Colleges, and the gradual
revolution which has been taking place has culminated in the
inauguration this season of Leinster College Championships in
Hurling and Football similar to those in operation in Munster.

74

The 26th November, 1910, will be a red-letter day in the annals
of our games, as it furnished a revelation of the great awaken-
ing in the Colleges. Magnificent in all respects was the meeting
of College representatives who assembled on that day in the
Shelbourne Hotel in Dublin.  The chair was occupied by the
Very Rev. John Doody, President of St. Kieran's College, Kil-
kenny, who had specially travelled up from Kilkenny for the
occasion.  Enthusiastically did he extol the merits of our
manly pastimes, and with like enthusiasm did Father Kelly, of
St. Mary’s College, Knockbeg, second Father Doody’s proposi-
tion for the inauguration of the Championships Representa-
tives were also present from St. Enda’s College, St. Patrick’s
Training College, Marlboro’ Training College, the College of
Science, the Albert Agricultural College, the National Univer-
sity College, and Trinity College. Communications expressing
willingness to join the competitions came from St. Peter’s Col-
lege, Wexford ; Dominican College, Newbridge; Good Counsel
College, New Ross, and Mount St. Joseph's College, Roscrea.
Surely a meeting such as this makes full amends for past
neglect!  The most satisfactory feature of all was the fact
that the movement came from within the Colleges themselves,
for the circular convening the meeting was issued from Professor
Doody, Ph.D, of St. Enda’s College. ~The officers of the
Governing Committee were elected as follows : —Chairman, the
Very Rev. J. Doody; Vice-Chairman, Mr. P. H. Pearse, BA,
B.L., the Founder and Principal of Szoit €anna; and Hon.
Secretary and Treasurer, Daithi de Burca, 4 Gardiner's Place.
Dublin.  The following are the principal conditions of the
Championships, but it is likely that a slight change may be
made in the age limit for the junior contests.

(a) The Championships will be governed by a committee
consisting of one representative of each College or College
club entering, together with a Patron, Chairman, Vice-
Chairman, and Hon. Secretary and Treasurer.

() Senior and Junior Championships will be held in
Hurling and Football, the Junior Championship to be open
to students under 18 years of age on 1st June next, and the
Senior Championships to be open without restriction to all
members of Colleges (students and staff).

75

(c) The entrance fee is 5s. per team, and the latest date
for entering is the 14th January next. -

(d) The champion teams in the respective grades will
have the right to represent the province in any All-Ireland
Inter-College Championships that may hereafter be held.

Verily, competitions ushered into existence under such aus-
pices are assured of a brilliant and successful future. The
Championships will be begun in the new year, and the promoters
are hopeful that their efforts will receive the co-operation of a
great many other Colleges which did not participate in the
meeting.

The inauguration of the Provincial Championship was, in
itself, a great day’s work, but the Shelbourne Hotel on that day
witnessed another event of like importance, when an Inter-
College League in Hurling and Football was initiated between
the Higher Colleges in Dublin.  The Committee of the League
has since met, and draws and fixtures for the competitions have
been made. Four Colleges are competing in the Hurling
League Championship, and a like number in the Football
League Championship. ~As a double round of matches will
be played, these contests will afford the Colleges plenty of
matches at times at which ordinary G.A.A. fixtures are not
available. The GE)CCX:S of the League are : —Patron, Dr. e
Coffey, President University College, Dublin; Chairman, Mr.
C. J. Walsh, Marlboro’ College; Vice-Chairman, Mr. b 15
Hegarty, College of Science; and Hon. Secretary and Treasurer,
Mr. F. J. Cronin (University College), Medical School, Cecilia
Street, Dublin. Practically all the higher Colleges are
entered, and certainly we have all those capable of exercising
the greatest influence. The University College brings us in
touch with the future professional and commercial men of the
country. The great Training Colleges for Teachers (St.
Patrick’s and Marlboro’) contain the National Teachers of to-
morrow.  Our future scientists are in training in the College
of Science, while our scientific agriculturists are being trained
in the Albert Agricultural College. Trinity College is not yet
with us—and that that is so is the result of the work of our
superior Colleges. But even there, an appeal by Captain the
Hon. Ottway Cuffe in favour of our National pastimes at the

76

inaugural meeting of the Gaelic Society was received with un-
qualified enthusiasm.

With so much accomplished, All-Ireland Championships are
now possible, and this year will, it is hoped, furnish the fulfil-
ment of our long-cherished aspirations in that direction. The
West is still asleep, and why ? ~ Surely, the University College,
in the heart of Gaeldom, recreates not after the fashion of the
alien! Galway has fine Secondary Colleges in the Ignatian
College and St. Joseph's Seminary in the city, and in the Col-
leges at Ballinasloe and Tuam in the county. Mayo contains
fine Colleges at Ballina and Castlebar, and there are the Bal-
laghadereen College in Roscommon, and the Summerhill
College in Sligo. These require but to be brought together, and
the establishment of a Provincial- College Championship is a
step easily taken. Awake then, Gaels of Connaught, and be
not behind the sister Provinces of Munster and Leinster in your
patriotic duty! Progressive Gaels of Ulster, you, too,
assuredly, will not be laggards when the rest of Ireland asks to
join hands with you!  Be up, then, and doing, and fail not to
enlist the support of the Colleges in your fight against alien
games.

Let us rejoice not too soon, however.  The fight is but begun.
But, at the same time, let us fully appreciate the fact that the
tide is turning in our favour. Safe in the conviction of the
superiority of our games, we can confidently rely on our boys
deciding in our favour if they get a fair chance of becoming
proficient in our games along with other games. Leaving the
Colleges expert in their native games, the boys will, on return-
ing to their native districts, utilise their influence in improving
local teams. The advent of all the educated youth into our
ranks will be productive of far-reaching benefits. We can look
forward then hopefully to the day when all classes will be
united in allegiance to their native pastimes, and under the
great Association of which we have the honour to be members.
To hasten the approach of that day, let all Gaels co-operate and
arouse themselves to a keener organisation of the forces in their
midst. By organisation we have secured our present position,
and let our watch-word in our onward march be, “ Organise !”

IRISH FOOTBALL SONGS.

L EINSTER seems to be the only province that can boast of
producing songs in the National language, commemorat-
ing football matches which took place in the north of the pro-
vince. Football seems to be an older game in Ireland than
most people think, for in the transactions of the Iberno-Celtic
Society (Vol. L) appears the following under the year 1712:
“James M‘Cuairt, or Courtney, commonly called Dall McCuairt,
a native of Criamhthan, in the county of Louth, flourished at
this time”  He was author of 29 songs and poems which are
mentioned in the work referred to, and one of these songs was
written “ on a great match of football played at Slane, on the
banks of the Boyne, between the young men of the counties of
Meath and Louth.” This song was published in the “ Gaelic
Journal ” some years ago, and begins ‘ Da haiseanta crordearmait
mo macnaiy a5 teacc anior —High-spirited and courageous,
were my friends coming up.”  The names of Maguire, O'Reilly,
White and Heeney are mentioned as being members of the team,
and George McCleary seemed to have been the captain of either
party, as the poet mentions him as nis.  These games of foot-
ball were evidently played in the stretch of country between
Lough Erne and the Irish Sea, that is in the counties of Fer-
managh, Cavan, Monaghan, Armagh, Louth, and the north of
the County Dublin. It seems to have been the custom of one
side of a river to play against the other side, or for those living
on the banks of one river to play those of another. For instance,
in the song alluded to above, the poet refers to one of the teams
as the “Nanny men” The Nanny water flows through Lower
Meath, and empties itself into the Irish Sea about foqtgﬂes
below the mouth of the Boyne, and the poet speaks of this team
in terms far from complimentary, which was evidently because
they were Anglicised and unable to speak Gaelic, like the
other team. In these games of football it is interesting to
learn, as may be seen from the song below, that, if any man
acted foully towards another, he was bound to give rdrad (or
satisfaction) at the end of the game by wrestling his accuser.

THE FOOTBALL MATCH OF BAVAN MEADOW.

21

There were sixteen of the hearty fellows of Omeath,

And they playing football in the meadow of Bavan,

From the beginning of mid-day till the black fall of the sun

They did not win a goal on either side.

There were two gay active bands who were expert at the ball

Moving Jike the cold wind of March, )

And there is not a queen under the sun who would see their
appearance,

But would fall in a twinkling in love with them

L
There was a brave, discreet youth of the race of Red Stephen
there,

My hero was active on the meadow,

The son of this house above* was there, and when the two would
go into collision, °

To his right or his left he would kick the ball.

When the ball would be taken from them two “snatchers”
would follow,

And the ball would be (taken) from them at that run,

Was it not nice and cheery to be on the hill of Tullagh then

Looking at my boys training?

* The son of Murphy, who composed the song.

_—

USE ONLY PATERSON’S IRISH MATCHES.

82

1IL

There was a youth named O’Neill who should have been first in
this story,

‘Who would kick the ball expertly in the mouth of the goal;

Charles McCourt was bounding there, he would give a kick in
time of need,

And would escape from a tight corner by his running.

There were two of the Marleys there as smart as the birds,

And the ball with them at their topmost speed,

There were two more coming against them as quickly as the
ball,

The best and the flower of the O'Hagans.

Connolly was there, who was renowned from Cavan to Lough
Erne,

And who would get out of a hard defence by running.

Duffy was there alone active in the mouth of danger,

And you could not find the likes of the two to Hagan’s town.

IY:

Now I wish to treat of this match as it happened,

With the bold fellows of the place as I have heard,

They knew full well the men not now mentioned

Who did not happen to be present then.

Only it is as late as it is I would go down beside the shore,
Myself and my heroes merrily,

Where every pretty maid doés be with white fingers and rings,
Having fallen in love with my boys. R

(In the preceding verse the poet has named some men who came
down from the mountains in this verse he says that time does not
permit him to tell those who came up from the sea-shore. Omeath
being situated at the foot of the Cooley mountains and on the
very shore of Carlingford Lough, we may conclude that the
match took place between the mountain men on one side and
the fishermen on the other).

83
V.

Did ye Lear the calamity that happened to us lately :

How Paddy of the mountain died off from his friends?

He didn't last sixteen days after the match on the meadow,

When death took him from us without delay ;

He was the decent accomplished fellow for friendship and good
worth,

Manliness, generosity and comeliness,

The women were all in love with him, the weak and strong were

attached to him—  « . s
He was the fair flower of the women and maidens in the village.

VL

When he came into the meadow, he threw off his shirt,

And he took the sway from Bavan;

When he came into the meadow he asked undeceitfully :
“Where is the ball or the goal ?”

They spoke out with eagerness not to meet each other,

As a boisterous day and a very wet day would meet;

“ Give over your tricks, and in spite of all that are in Ireland
I will not leave the meadow without satisfaction.”

In both these poerns the words batta and téatpéro are used
indifferently for the English “ball”  The word Léana or
Leanard was the term used by both writers for the field in which
the football match took place, and tucc Fuavait, which occurs
in Verse 1L, is applied to those who take the ball from the others,
and then clear along the field with it.

Séamas McCuairt’s poem on the Boyne match is by far a better
one than that given above, both in metre, vocabulary and general
idiom, but it is altogether too long for publication. O'Reilly
says in his “Irish Writers” that it consisted of eighty-eight
verses, but only twelve are given in the “ Gaelic Journal” The
object of drawing attention to these two poems is to show that
the history of football in Ireland did not commence with Soccer
or Rugby, but was well established about the middle of the
seventeenth century.

A player who has competed in 1907, 1908 or 1909 Senior Co.
Championship, or 1907, 1908, 19og or 1910 Inter-County First
Division Championship, shall not be eligible to play Second
Division if his county has a First Division team of the same kind
as that in which he played.

Leinster 1908 and 1909 Second Division Hurling Champion-
ships shall not be eligible to compete in the 1910 Second Divi-

who won the Leinster 1908 and 1909 Second Division Football

« WEST LIMERICK HURLING AND FOOTBALL
LEAGUE.

The above League was established at a meeting of the Gaels
of West Limerick, held at Newcastle West on September sth,
1gog. Its influence as a factor in the revival of the.NaIEmnal
Games of Hurling and Football, during the brief period it 'has
been in existence, is most gratifying. Strong, vigomus, ﬁoun_sh»
ing Clubs have sprung into being where previously noth{ng
but apathy and “deadly dulness ” prevailed. The following
Clubs have already affiliated to the League : —Monegay, Rath-
keale, Ballingarry, Newcastle West, Castlemahon, Croagh,
Kilfinny, Kilcoleman, Knockaderry ; while Clubs are in course
of formation in Drumcollogher, Fohenagh, Ardagh, etc.

The League Committee is composed of two representatives
from each affiliated Club, with the following officers : —

President—Mr. J. A. Collins, Newcastle West.
Vice-President—Mr. P. Purcell, Croagh.
Treasurer—Mr. H. McDonnell, Ballingarry.
Registrar—Mr. Tim Sheehan, Monegay.

. Hon. Sec—Mr. Thos. Reidy, Ballingarry.

121

The competitions, which will be carried out on the single
League system, were started at Knockaderry on April 10th, when
Ballingarry defeated Castlemahon. On the following Sunday
Croagh defeated Knockaderry in Ballingarry. In this way
the competition will be continued during the season, providing
healthy, virile, invigorating amusement in the ‘different centres
embraced, the claims of each of which will receive attention in
turn. Judging from the enthusiasm which the League has
uogsed, its success is likely to exceed the most sanguine
anticipations.

[This League is a much-needed evidence of G.A. vitality and
enterprise in Co. Limerick.—ED.]

President William H. Taft, in acknowledging an invitation
to accept the position of a Patron of the United States Pro-
vincial Council, wrote : —“ I beg to acknowledge receipt of your
favour of the 23rd of August, inviting me to become a Patron
of the United States Provincial Council of the Gaelic Athletic
Association of Ireland, and, in reply, to thank you for the
honour of the invitation, which I have pleasure in accepting.”
